title: Kafka Internals & Stream Processing Deep Dive
description: Apache Kafka internals beyond the basics for SAP engineers. Covers partition assignment strategy, consumer group rebalancing, exactly-once semantics internals, log compaction, storage architecture, KRaft mode, Kafka Connect architecture, stream processing with Kafka Streams, and how SAP Event Mesh relates to native Kafka.

sections.title: Kafka Architecture — From Event Mesh to Native Kafka

description.value:
SAP Event Mesh is a managed Kafka-compatible messaging service on BTP. It provides a subset of Kafka's capabilities (topic creation, publishing, consuming) but abstracts away Kafka's internal complexity. At FAANG, engineers work with native Kafka and need to understand what Event Mesh hides: brokers, partitions, replication, ISR (in-sync replicas), controller election, and rack awareness.

Broker architecture: A Kafka cluster consists of multiple brokers (servers). Each broker stores some topic partitions. One broker is the controller (manages partition leadership, handles broker failures). In KRaft mode (Kafka 3.x+), the controller is a Kafka process itself; in older versions, ZooKeeper handled controller election. Each partition has one leader broker (handles all reads/writes) and N follower brokers (replicate the leader's data). SAP analogy: broker = application server instance, partition leader = dispatcher for a specific work process type.

Partition replication: Replication factor N means each partition's data is stored on N brokers. The in-sync replica (ISR) set contains brokers that are fully caught up with the leader. If the leader fails, a new leader is elected from the ISR. This is the mechanism that ensures Kafka's durability guarantees. Compare to SAP: an SAP system's database replication (HANA System Replication) serves a similar purpose, but Kafka's replication is per-partition, not per-system.

Topic configuration: Key Kafka topic settings include: partitions (parallelism level), replication factor (durability), retention.ms (how long messages are kept), cleanup.policy (delete or compact), min.insync.replicas (minimum ISR for write acknowledgment). Event Mesh sets these for you; FAANG engineers configure them explicitly based on their requirements.

Kafka producers are more sophisticated than SAP queue senders (SMQ1). Understanding producer internals is essential for writing performant and reliable producers.

Batching: Kafka producers batch messages before sending to the broker. The batch.size parameter controls the maximum batch size (default 16KB). linger.ms controls how long to wait for a batch to fill (default 0 — send immediately). Larger batches = higher throughput but higher latency. This is like SAP's tRFC queue batching but configurable and with different tradeoffs. If you send 10,000 messages with batch.size=64KB and linger.ms=10, you'll get fewer, larger requests to the broker — more efficient but introduces 10ms delay.

Compression: Kafka supports gzip, snappy, lz4, and zstd compression. Compression happens on the producer side (CPU cost) and decompression on the consumer side. Network-bound workloads benefit significantly from compression (50-80% size reduction). Snappy is the most common choice — good compression, low CPU overhead. This is similar to SAP's compression in RFC data transmission but configurable at the topic or producer level.

Idempotent producers (enable.idempotence=true): This is how Kafka achieves exactly-once semantics in the produce path. The producer assigns a unique Producer ID (PID) and sequence number to each message. The broker deduplicates messages with the same PID and sequence number. If a producer sends a message, doesn't receive the acknowledgment, retries, and sends the same message again — the broker detects the duplicate (same PID + sequence number) and ignores it. This guarantees no duplicates even with retries, at the cost of a small performance overhead. SAP's equivalent: IDoc status management ensures idempotent processing, but at the application level, not the protocol level.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Kafka Producer Batching Tradeoff: Throughput vs Latency

variables.name: network_cost
variables.description: Network cost per request (plus data xfer)

calculation.formula: Number of requests per second = (msg_count / 86400) / (batch_size / msg_size). Network cost = requests × network_cost_per_request + data xfer cost.
calculation.cp: lingerms=0: 578 requests/sec, 50M requests/day × $0.01 = $500/day network cost. P99 latency: 2000ms.
calculation.ap: lingerms=100: 5 requests/sec, 432K requests/day × $0.01 = $4.3/day network cost. P99 latency: 2100ms.
calculation.breakeven: Batching reduces network cost by 99% ($500/day vs $4.3/day) at the cost of 100ms additional latency. For most real-time use cases, 100ms is negligible. For ultra-low-latency use cases (payment processing, fraud detection), zero batching may be worth $500/day. This is a classic Staff+ tradeoff: understand your latency requirements before optimizing network cost.

description.value:
Consumer groups are Kafka's mechanism for distributing message processing across multiple consumers. Understanding consumer group rebalancing is critical because it's the most common source of production issues at FAANG — and the area where Event Mesh users have the least experience (Event Mesh handles this automatically).

How consumer groups work: A consumer group has one or more consumers sharing a group.id. Each partition of a subscribed topic is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. If you have 10 partitions and 3 consumers, each consumer processes 3-4 partitions. If a consumer fails, its partitions are reassigned to the remaining consumers. This is called rebalancing. SAP analogy: a work process pool with dynamic assignment — if one work process crashes, its requests are reassigned to other work processes.

Types of rebalancing: (1) Cooperative rebalancing (incremental): the consumer signals 'I'm going to release partitions X and Y' before the rebalance completes. This allows sticky assignment and minimal disruption. (2) Eager rebalancing (stop-the-world): ALL consumers stop processing, revoke ALL partitions, then receive new assignments. This causes a complete processing pause (seconds to minutes). Since Kafka 3.1, cooperative rebalancing is the default. If you see a production issue where 'consumers stopped processing for 30 seconds,' it's likely an eager rebalance caused by a consumer timing out.

Static group membership: Use group.instance.id to give each consumer a unique, static name. This prevents rebalances when a consumer restarts (the group knows the consumer will come back). Without static membership, every consumer start/stop triggers a rebalance. This is a must-use for any production deployment.

SA P-specific: Event Mesh manages consumer groups for you, hiding the complexity of partition assignment and rebalancing. At FAANG, you configure these explicitly. Always use: cooperative rebalancing (partition.assignment.strategy=CooperativeStickyAssignor), static group membership (group.instance.id), and session.timeout.ms=45000 (45 seconds gives time for GC pauses without triggering false rebalances).

sections.title: Kafka Connect and Stream Processing (Kafka Streams)

description.value:
Kafka is not just a message broker — it's a streaming platform. Two of the most important components built on Kafka are Kafka Connect (for integrating with external systems) and Kafka Streams (for stream processing). Understanding these concepts is essential for FAANG engineers working with event-driven architectures.

Kafka Connect: A framework for connecting Kafka with external systems (databases, storage systems, APIs). Source connectors read from external systems and write to Kafka. Sink connectors read from Kafka and write to external systems. Common connectors: Debezium (CDC from databases), JDBC (read/write databases), S3 (store Kafka messages in S3). SAP analogy: PI/PO adapter engine. PI/PO has adapters for IDoc, RFC, SOAP, FILE, JDBC. Kafka Connect has equivalent connectors but is open source and community-driven. The key difference: Kafka Connect runs as a distributed service (like a Kafka broker) with automatic failover and rebalancing.

Debezium — Change Data Capture (CDC): Debezium is a Kafka Connect source connector that captures row-level changes from databases (MySQL, Postgres, MongoDB, Oracle, SQL Server). It reads the database's transaction log (binary log, WAL) and produces change events to Kafka. This is the FAANG equivalent of SAP's ALE/IDoc change propagation. Instead of business objects writing IDocs, the database itself produces change events. The advantage: zero changes to the application code — Debezium is transparent to the database client. SAP analogy: ALE is application-level CDC (business objects produce IDocs); Debezium is infrastructure-level CDC (database produces events).

Kafka Streams: A stream processing library that runs in your application (no separate cluster needed). It provides: stateful processing (aggregations, joins, windowing), exactly-once semantics (end-to-end), fault-tolerant state stores (RocksDB backed by Kafka topics), and a DSL for stream transformations. SAP analogy: ABAP internal table operations (LOOP, COLLECT, SORT, DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES) on streaming data. Kafka Streams is like having the power of SQL GROUP BY and JOIN applied to infinite, real-time data streams. FAANG uses Kafka Streams for: real-time fraud detection, sessionization, anomaly detection, data enrichment, and materialized views.

decisionTree.title: When to Use Kafka Connect vs Kafka Streams vs Custom Consumers
decisionTree.prompt: You need to get data from a Postgres database into Kafka, transform it, and write the result to another system. Which Kafka component should you use?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "What is the source of the data?" If it's a database (Postgres, MySQL, Oracle), use Debezium (Kafka Connect source connector) for CDC. If it's an HTTP API, use Kafka Connect REST source connector or a custom producer. If it's an existing message queue (RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ), use a Kafka Connect source connector for that queue.

branches.condition: Source is a database and you want real-time change capture without application changes
branches.path: Use Debezium (Kafka Connect). It reads the database transaction log and produces change events. No application code changes needed. Best for: replicating data, auditing, synchronization.

branches.condition: You need to transform, filter, or aggregate the data in Kafka before writing to a sink
branches.path: Use Kafka Streams. It's a lightweight library that runs in your application. Supports stateful operations (aggregations, joins, windowing). Best for: real-time processing, enrichment, materialized views.

branches.condition: You need to write Kafka data to an external data store (S3, Elasticsearch, HDFS)
branches.path: Use Kafka Connect Sink connectors. They handle exactly-once delivery and schema evolution. Best for: exporting data, loading into data warehouses, building data lakes.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Most SAP engineers default to building custom consumers because they're used to writing ABAP programs to process IDocs. At FAANG, always prefer Kafka Connect for import/export and Kafka Streams for processing. Custom consumers should only be used when you need application-specific logic that can't be expressed as stateless transformations or when you need tight integration with your service's business logic.

details: Staff+ Insight: Kafka is the single most important infrastructure component at FAANG that SAP engineers need to understand deeply. If Event Mesh is your starting point, think of it as 'Kafka with training wheels' — Event Mesh handles consumer group management, schema registry, and client configuration for you. At FAANG, you configure all of these explicitly. The most common production issues at FAANG involve: consumer group rebalancing (especially eager rebalancing causing processing pauses), producer idempotency misconfiguration (resulting in duplicate messages), and partition skew (uneven distribution of data across partitions). Run a 3-broker Kafka cluster locally, produce 1M messages, simulate a broker failure, and observe the rebalance. This exercise alone will teach you more than reading Kafka documentation for a month.
